data.title: RFK Jr. to fire all members of Obamacare task force: WSJ
data.description:
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is looking to oust all members of the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force, a key regulatory component introduced under the Affordable Care Act, also known as Obamacare, in 2010, The Wall Street Journal reported.

While USPSTF was established in 1984, under provisions of the ACA, it determines which preventive health services, such as cancer screenings and HIV treatments, that private health insurance plans should cover at zero cost to patients.

Citing people familiar with the matter, The Journal reported that RFK Jr. plans to remove all 16 members of the panel because he considers them too “woke.”

HHS refused to comment on his plans for the task force or his views on its membership. “The Secretary looks forward to working with the USPSTF to improve public health,” a spokesperson for RFK Jr. said.

The report comes weeks after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in favor of the federal government in a case challenging the constitutionality of the appointments made by the HHS Secretary to the USPSTF.

Days after the ruling, RFK Jr. postponed a July 10 meeting of the panel, scheduled to discuss healthy food intake, physical activity, and other measures that can prevent cardiovascular disease.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump's Fed visit a 'welcomed light-hearted meeting' as Powell 'strengthened' independence
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump toured the Federal Reserve this week in the first instance of an incumbent leader visiting the central bank since 2006.

Despite some visible squabbling with chair Jerome Powell over the construction costs of the Fed's new headquarters and Trump's repeated insistence to lower interest rates, the visit was welcomed by market participants after weeks of mounting concerns.

Trump has kept up a barrage of criticism against Powell almost ever since he took office, chastising the Fed chief for not lowering interest rates and calling him names such as "numbskull." Meanwhile, media reports have said Trump has speculated firing Powell before his term ends in May 2026, sparking mounting concerns over the independence of the Fed. 

Trump told reporters on Friday that Powell is "a very good man."

"I think we had a very good meeting on interest rates... He said to me, very strongly, 'The country is doing well' ... And I got that to mean that I think he's going to start recommending lower rates," Trump said. 

During Trump's visit to the Fed, the president was asked whether there was anything Powell could say to him that would make him back off from some of his earlier criticism.

"Well, I'd love him to lower interest rates," Trump had said with a light pat on Powell's back that was met with laughter.

"I think it was a welcomed light-hearted meeting between (Trump and Powell). In classic Trump style, the two men donning hard hats was a made-for-TV moment, and the president's friendly slap on Powell's back countered harsh Truth Social posts directed at the Fed chief recently," Seeking Alpha analyst Mike Zaccardi said.

Chris Lau, investing group leader of DIY Value Investing, also weighed in: "(Powell) conducted himself professionally, factually, and stayed on point with the funds spent on the current and completed constructions. With the FOMC's independence already assured, he strengthened those optics."  

"The Fed said they were 'honored' to host President Trump, so the temperature has clearly been turned down. We'll see what Powell has to say next Wednesday when he holds his usual FOMC press conference. The Jackson Hole Economic Symposium comes next, which could be used as a stage to tee up a September rate cut," Zaccardi added. 

The Fed's monetary policy committee does indeed meet next week to issue its fifth interest rate decision of the year. Traders expect the Fed to hold its key rate steady as policymakers continue to wait and watch for the impact of tariffs to show up on consumer prices. 

"(Trump) reasserted that 'too late Powell' was too late in cutting interest rates. However, the FOMC cuts rates when inflation is downward. The time is ticking for tariffs, or consumption taxes paid through importers, on Americans to show up in future CPI reports," Lau added. 

Deutsche Bank's head of FX research, George Saravelos, earlier this month said a potential ouster of Powell by Trump could "collapse" both the currency and bond markets.

Wall Street put in a strong showing this week, with the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) notching its 14th record close of the year. Sentiment was boosted by positive trade developments and solid quarterly reports from some of the world's largest companies.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Elon Musk’s Neuralink joins bionic eye study
data.description:
Neuralink, the neurotechnology company co-founded by Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO Elon Musk, has joined a clinical trial aimed at restoring vision with full or partial blindness through visual prostheses, commonly known as bionic eyes.

According to an update posted on the federal register for clinical trials this week, the study named “AI-powered artificial vision for visual prostheses” is expected to enroll seven adults, including “Neuralink patients (once available).”

The study, sponsored by the University of California, Santa Barbara, has three primary objectives, including pattern discrimination accuracy and scene understanding performance in subjects implanted with a visual prosthesis.

Led by Dr. Michael Beyeler, who directs UC Santa Barbara’s Bionic Vision Lab, the trial includes the Spanish university Universidad Miguel Hernández de Elche and California-based Neuralink as its collaborators.

The update, first reported by Bloomberg News, comes at a time when Neuralink is running two U.S.-based clinical trials, PRIME and CONVOY, designed to study its brain-computer interface in patients with limb paralysis.

The company has yet to launch U.S. clinical trials for its brain chip, Blindsight, which aims to restore vision in people with blindness.

Musk, who also leads internet service provider Starlink (STRLK) and space exploration company SpaceX (SPACE), previously said that Neuralink aims to implant Blindsight in a human for the first time later this year.

data.title: Sarepta denies Elevidys is linked to latest patient death in Brazil
data.description:
Shortly after the U.S. FDA announced an investigation into the death of a patient who had received Elevidys, a gene therapy developed by Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT), the company said that the death reported in Brazil is unrelated to its treatment.

Sarepta (NASDAQ:SRPT) shares extended losses in the post-market trading on Friday after the FDA said it is investigating the death of a patient with Duchenne muscular dystrophy who had died on June 7 after receiving Elevidys.

“The death of this patient was deemed unrelated to treatment with Elevidys,” the company said, citing a report on Bloomberg News. Sarepta (NASDAQ:SRPT) added that its Swiss partner Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), which markets Elevidys outside the U.S., has also declared that the death was not related to Elevidys.

The latest death reported from Elevidys-treated patients comes at a time when SRPT has already paused U.S. shipments of the drug following three earlier deaths linked to the treatment and another gene therapy developed by the company.

data.title: Carter’s targets price increases and store optimization amid $125M–$150M annual tariff headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Carter's, Inc. (CRI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Douglas C. Palladini emphasized that after 100 days in the role, he has "a clear picture of what success will look like as we move forward, returning all Carter's brands to growth that is long-term, sustainable and profitable." He highlighted renewed focus on product newness, consumer connectivity, and a revamped leadership team with new appointments in retail, strategy, and marketing.
  • Palladini stated Carter’s will "regain lost market share and reinforce Carter’s position as the #1 baby apparel brand in all points of distribution and across all vital product categories," with baby category sales growing 10% in Q2 year-over-year. He also announced the launch of Otter Avenue, a new toddler-focused brand, and indicated plans to close about 100 underperforming stores as leases expire.
  • CFO Richard F. Westenberger reported, "Sales in the second quarter were $585 million… Our Q2 reported operating income was $4 million." He noted that adjusted operating income and earnings per share were down considerably due to planned investments in retail pricing and higher SG&A, and described tariffs as a "significant uncertainty and challenge to planning our business."

Outlook

  • Westenberger confirmed Carter’s has not reinstated forward-looking guidance, citing "the overall uncertainty around tariffs and their potential impact on the business." He projected a net earnings impact of approximately $35 million in the second half of 2025 from incremental tariffs, with an estimated annualized gross tariff increase of $125 million to $150 million.
  • Management indicated that average unit retail prices (AURs) are planned to be up low single digits in the second half, versus down about 4% in the first half. Carter’s intends to "raise our prices" to offset tariffs, and expects a "more meaningful impact" from tariffs in future quarters.
  • CFO Westenberger stated, “In 2026 and beyond, our planning assumption is that we’ll be able to offset these costs,” signaling planned mitigation through price increases, vendor and wholesale partner cost-sharing, and production shifts.

Financial Results

  • Second quarter sales reached $585 million, a 4% increase from the prior year, driven by U.S. Retail and International segments. Adjusted operating income was $12 million (2% margin), with adjusted EPS at $0.17. Reported operating income was $4 million, including $8 million in charges.
  • Gross margin in Q2 was 48.1%, down 200 basis points due to retail price investments, higher off-price inventory sales, and inbound freight rates. SG&A rose by $26 million (10%), mainly from increased store-related expenses and a larger store base.
  • International sales saw strong growth, with Canada up 8% and Mexico up 19% in comparable sales. Inventory ended Q2 up 3% year-over-year, with $17 million attributed to higher tariff costs.

Q&A

  • Jay Daniel Sole, UBS: Asked about long-term sales and earnings targets. Palladini: "We have substantial and meaningful reasons to believe that we can return to growth that…can be profitable and sustained…But we're not going to share specific numbers today."
  • Sole followed up on tariff mitigation. Westenberger: "Probably the most meaningful opportunity is price…That's not the only tool…We have good partnership with our vendors. We are sharing some of the…tariff costs with our wholesale partners."
  • Kelly Crago, Citi: Inquired about store closures and DTC strategy. Palladini: "All options are on the table, closing more stores, moving stores, opening new stores, relocating, remodeling…We believe there's a tremendous opportunity there."
  • Crago also pressed on pricing flexibility. Westenberger: "We have begun to raise prices…Dialogue with our wholesale customers has been very constructive…We have probably more flexibility in our own retail channel."
  • James Andrew Chartier, Monness, Crespi & Hardt: Asked about investment levels. Palladini refrained from quantifying but emphasized high MROI, especially in store traffic and consumer loyalty.
  • Christopher Michael Nardone, BofA: Probed on category weakness and competition. Palladini cited increased private label competition but underscored Carter's leadership and global brand awareness. Westenberger highlighted opportunities for expanding Little Planet and Otter Avenue in wholesale.
  • Irwin Bernard Boruchow, Wells Fargo: Asked which channel would outperform in the back half. Westenberger pointed to direct-to-consumer as the main growth driver, with higher second-half revenue growth planned.
  • Paul David Kearney, Barclays: Questioned AUR increases and margin offsets. Westenberger said the $35 million tariff headwind is the net effect after price and other mitigations. Kearney also asked about SG&A shifts, with Westenberger citing store closures and ongoing productivity programs.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing for details on margin bottoms, tariff offsets, and growth clarity, while repeatedly seeking quantification and specific targets management declined to provide.
  • Management’s tone was cautiously optimistic in prepared remarks, shifting to defensive and non-quantitative in Q&A. Palladini and Westenberger avoided giving specific forward numbers, using phrases like "we're not going to share specific numbers today" and "I really don't think that there's much more detail we can provide."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone showed increased confidence in strategic direction and mitigation efforts, but both analysts and executives continued to focus on tariff risk and recovery timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains suspended, with management reiterating uncertainty primarily due to tariffs, consistent with last quarter.
  • Strategic focus has shifted toward more aggressive price increases, store optimization, and new brand launches (Otter Avenue) compared to Q1, with explicit plans to close about 100 stores as leases expire.
  • Key metric changes include a 4% year-over-year sales growth in Q2 versus a 5% decline in Q1, and baby category growth accelerating from 5% in Q1 to 10% in Q2.
  • Analysts pressed more vigorously for specifics on tariff mitigation and margin floors, while management maintained a similar level of cautious optimism but offered greater detail on tariff action plans and new brand development.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs present the most prominent risk, with management estimating a $125 million to $150 million annualized gross impact, and a $35 million net earnings impact for the second half of 2025.
  • Management cited uncertainty around consumer response to higher prices, additional reciprocal tariffs, and the overall consumer sentiment as key risks.
  • Mitigation strategies include price increases, vendor and wholesale partner negotiations, diversification of supply chain, and ongoing cost controls.
  • Store productivity and SG&A remain under scrutiny, with planned store closures and efforts to transfer sales to remaining locations.

Final Takeaway

Carter's management signaled that foundational work is translating into business momentum, with stabilization in core categories and growth in baby and international segments. Facing substantial tariff headwinds, the company is advancing price increases, supply chain agility, and store optimization, while launching new brands and enhancing consumer connectivity. Leadership emphasized a return to sustainable, profitable growth, but refrained from providing specific forward guidance due to ongoing market uncertainty and tariff developments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bel Fuse signals sequential growth with Q3 sales guidance of $165M–$180M backed by strong Q2 bookings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bel Fuse Inc. (BELFB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Farouq Tuweiq, President and CEO, stated the company was "very pleased with our second quarter performance, which surpassed our revenue expectations and delivered gross margins at the higher end of our projected range." He highlighted an uptick in intra-quarter turns, especially in Power and Magnetics, and pointed to end market performance in commercial air, defense, and networking as drivers. Tuweiq described the business as "heading into recovery as we have been anticipating following nearly 2 years of inventory destocking in the channel."
  • He addressed the impact of tariffs, explaining that "tariffs had a limited impact in the second quarter, accounting for about $2 million of our sales and having a minimal effect on margins."
  • Looking forward, Tuweiq projected "sales guidance in the range of $165 million to $180 million and gross margins projected between 37% and 39%" for Q3, with strong Q2 bookings supporting expectations for sequential growth through the year.
  • CFO Lynn Hutkin reported, "Sales for the second quarter of 2025 reached $168.3 million, reflecting an increase of 26.3% from the second quarter of 2024." She detailed strong aerospace and defense (A&D) performance, Power Solutions and Protection sales of $86.8 million, and Connectivity Solutions sales of $59.2 million. Hutkin also noted that the company used $30 million of cash to repay long-term debt and generated $20.7 million in cash flow from operating activities during the quarter.

Outlook

  • Tuweiq shared optimism for continued growth, stating "Looking ahead to the third quarter, we are optimistic about continued growth with sales guidance in the range of $165 million to $180 million and gross margins projected between 37% and 39%." He described strong bookings as reinforcing confidence in sequential growth for the rest of the year.
  • Hutkin commented that the gross margin guidance for Q3 "does factor in some potential downward pressure related to FX," specifically mentioning currency exposure to the Chinese renminbi, Mexican peso, and Israeli shekel.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the Q3 sales guidance marks a substantial increase from the Q2 guidance range of $145 million to $155 million, reflecting improved clarity and momentum.

Financial Results

  • Sales for Q2 2025 were $168.3 million, up 26.3% from Q2 2024. Power Solutions and Protection contributed $86.8 million, with growth largely driven by aerospace and defense exposure. Connectivity Solutions reached $59.2 million, and Magnetics Solutions recorded $22.3 million.
  • Gross margin for the Power segment was 41.9%. Connectivity Solutions achieved a gross margin of 39.2%, and Magnetics Solutions improved to 28.7%. R&D expenses were $8.1 million, and SG&A expenses totaled $30.9 million or 18.4% of sales.
  • The company finished Q2 with $59.3 million in cash and securities, used $30 million for long-term debt repayment, and generated $20.7 million in operating cash flow.

Q&A

  • Robert Brooks, Northland Capital Markets, asked about trends underpinning the guidance and whether growth was coming from returning customers or new wins. Hutkin responded that the rebound in networking and distribution was driving orders, while Tuweiq added "you need a return to growth both from your OEMs and your disti." He emphasized, "we do see some new business, and we had some really nice new wins in the quarter, programs in our aerospace defense business."
  • Brooks inquired about margin initiatives and the sale of the Glen Rock facility. Tuweiq explained the sale aimed to drive "margin improvements and drive efficiencies within the Connectivity business," and confirmed there are no current plans for further facility sales.
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer, probed the improved intra-quarter order turns. Tuweiq noted, "we are seeing heading into the quarter and not having orders and then all of a sudden, the order comes in, we ship it out within the quarter. So that is nice to see because that indicates a little bit more healthiness in the channel and overall the market."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham & Company, asked about Power Solutions gross margins. Hutkin clarified, "the growth was related not to Enercon sequentially. They were flat quarter-over-quarter from Q1, but it was the legacy Power business, which historically is a lower margin product group than the recently acquired Enercon business."
  • Greg Palm, Craig-Hallum Capital, inquired about paused revenue from China. Hutkin shared, "about 2/3 of it ultimately got shipped in the second quarter and the balance is expected to go out in the third quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on the sustainability of recovery, risks from tariffs, and the nature of sequential growth. Their questions were probing but balanced, with a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing recovery, robust bookings, and operational discipline. During Q&A, Tuweiq and Hutkin provided detailed, transparent responses and acknowledged ongoing risks, using confident language such as "we are optimistic about continued growth" and "we remain confident in our ability to deliver value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment shifted from uncertainty about tariffs and demand to a more confident outlook, while analysts continued to probe for risks and sustainability of trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance improved from Q1, with higher sales expectations and a more positive outlook on inventory destocking and end market recovery.
  • Strategic focus shifted from uncertainty around tariffs and the impact on demand to driving operational efficiencies and leveraging new business wins, particularly in aerospace and defense and networking.
  • Analysts' focus moved from tariff-related disruptions and demand clarity to the sources of sequential growth, margin sustainability, and the effect of intra-quarter order turns.
  • Management's confidence increased markedly, using more optimistic language regarding growth and margin prospects, while analysts remained attentive to risks but acknowledged improved momentum.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing variables related to tariffs, stating "there are still many variables at play regarding tariffs, and we will continue to adapt to this evolving landscape."
  • Currency exposure to the Chinese renminbi, Mexican peso, and Israeli shekel was noted, with hedging programs in place but potential for downward pressure on gross margins.
  • Challenges in the consumer, rail, and e-mobility end markets were cited as offsets to growth in other segments.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of inventory rebuild, margin sustainability, and the full resolution of Chinese supplier issues. Management indicated progress on rebuilding the supplier base and expects comps to normalize in Q3.

Final Takeaway

Bel Fuse Inc. reported a robust second quarter, surpassing revenue expectations and achieving high gross margins, with notable growth in aerospace, defense, and networking. Management projects continued sequential growth, guiding Q3 sales between $165 million and $180 million, supported by strong bookings and improving market health. While tariffs and FX pressures remain ongoing risks, management asserts confidence in its operational strategies, new business wins, and ability to deliver value through the remainder of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hexcel outlines $1B cash generation target through 2029 as A350 and defense demand strengthen
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hexcel Corporation (HXL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas C. Gentile stated that "The fundamentals for the commercial aerospace industry and for Hexcel's outlook continue to be very positive," highlighting strong order books at Airbus and Boeing. Gentile discussed the company's extensive portfolio in advanced lightweight composite materials and its pivotal role in supporting record new commercial aircraft orders and growing defense spending.
  • Gentile noted positive momentum for Boeing's 737 MAX, now at a production rate of 38 aircraft per month, and progress on the 787 as it moves toward 7 aircraft per month in 2025. He indicated Airbus A320 ramp optimism for the second half of 2025 and a production target of 75 aircraft per month on the A320neo program by 2027.
  • Gentile identified the A350 as the company's largest and most challenging program, citing "destocking impact in Europe in the second quarter based on high levels of inventory," with destocking expected to continue through Q3 and normalize by Q4. He stated, "Each monthly step-up in the monthly A350 build rate brings significant revenue and operating leverage benefits for Hexcel."
  • New and renewed partnerships were announced, including a preferred supplier agreement with Embraer, a long-term supply deal with Kongsberg, and a collaboration with FLYING WHALES to develop advanced airship structures using Hexcel's carbon fiber.
  • Gentile reported Q2 2025 sales of $490 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.50 per share. He emphasized, "We expect to generate over $1 billion of cash cumulatively over the next 4 years."
  • Gentile also referenced a $50 million share repurchase in Q2, bringing total repurchases to $100 million for the year and $350 million over the past 18 months.
  • CFO Patrick Joseph Winterlich said, "Commercial aerospace sales of $293 million decreased 8.9% compared to the second quarter of 2024." He detailed defense, space, and other sales of $197 million, up 7.6% from the prior year, and noted, "Gross margin of 22.8% in the second quarter of 2025 decreased from 25.3% in the second quarter of 2024 as lower sales and inventory reduction actions negatively impacted operating leverage."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed 2025 guidance, with Gentile stating, "We continue to remain extremely vigilant on the internal elements of our business that we can control, such as on-time delivery."
  • Winterlich commented, "We are reaffirming our 2025 guidance with the caveat that we are still reviewing the recent change to tax laws."
  • Gentile projected, "We expect that we will generate over $1 billion of cash flow in the next 4 years."
  • The company expects continued tariff impact of $3 million to $4 million per quarter, with ongoing mitigation efforts.

Financial Results

  • Hexcel reported Q2 2025 sales of $489.9 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.50 per share.
  • Commercial aerospace accounted for approximately 60% of total Q2 sales, with $293 million, while defense, space, and other represented about 40%, or $196.8 million.
  • Gross margin for Q2 was 22.8%, down from 25.3% in the same quarter last year. The decrease was attributed to lower operating leverage from reduced sales volume and inventory actions.
  • Adjusted operating income in Q2 was $54.2 million, or 11.1% of sales; composite materials segment margin was 14.1%; engineered products segment margin was 10.9%, excluding Belgium closure impact.
  • Net cash used by operating activities in the first 6 months of 2025 was $5.2 million. Free cash flow for the period was negative $46.6 million. The company repurchased $50.5 million of stock in Q2.
  • The Board declared a $0.17 quarterly dividend.

Q&A

  • Kenneth George Herbert, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about A350 production rate assumptions for the year. Gentile responded that the plan was adjusted from 84 to 68 units, now expecting "something in the low 60s for the full year," with destocking expected to end in Q3 and a stronger Q4.
  • David Egon Strauss, Barclays: Inquired about A350 effective shipping rates during H1. Gentile said, "In the first quarter, the rates were kind of in the low 6s and then in the second quarter, in the high 5s," noting the impact of destocking.
  • Gautam J. Khanna, TD Cowen: Asked about the recoupling pace for A350. Gentile indicated Hexcel expects to be "closely and more coupled" with Airbus in 2026. Khanna also queried on 787 outlook; Gentile expects a stronger second half.
  • Myles Alexander Walton, Wolfe Research: Sought clarification on using the Airbus supplier award in contract discussions. Gentile explained that although contracts are long-term, "we're constantly working productivity initiatives where we can jointly share the benefit."
  • Michael Frank Ciarmoli, Truist Securities: Asked about tariff headwinds and earnings guidance. Gentile said, "Our EPS target is to $1.95 or $195. And so there could be some pressure that brings us towards the lower end of the range if the full tariff impact hits."
  • Richard Tobie Safran, Seaport Research Partners: Probed on defense spending's long-term impact. Gentile emphasized, "We see that probably as our top core organic growth opportunity is defense, both in the U.S. and in Europe."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally neutral to slightly cautious, focusing on the pace of destocking, margin recovery, tariff impacts, and defense growth sustainability. Several questions pressed for clarity on A350 rates and the ability to offset margin headwinds, reflecting ongoing supply chain uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Gentile stating, "We are extremely confident that with Hexcel's unrivaled portfolio of technology and lightweight product offerings... there is a great opportunity for Hexcel to generate strong incremental margins." In Q&A, management was forthcoming about headwinds but reiterated confidence in demand recovery and operational execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained resolute but acknowledged continued near-term challenges, particularly with A350 destocking and margin pressures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 was reaffirmed, consistent with the prior quarter, but management flagged ongoing review due to tax law changes and tariff uncertainties.
  • Strategic focus remained on cost control, operational efficiency, and long-term cash flow generation, echoing last quarter’s themes.
  • Key metric changes included a sequential revenue increase and defense sales acceleration, while gross margin declined further from Q1’s 22.4% to 22.8% in Q2.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward defense segment growth, the end of A350 destocking, margin trajectory, and the timing of supply chain normalization.
  • Management’s confidence in long-term cash generation and incremental margin expanded, with continued attention to operational excellence and footprint optimization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Supply chain disruption and destocking on the A350 continued to impact results, with Gentile warning that destocking would persist through Q3.
  • Management cited tariffs as a growing cost headwind, forecasting $3 million to $4 million per quarter, and highlighted the uncertainty of further tariff changes.
  • Gross margin compression due to lower sales and reduced overhead absorption was noted as a key challenge.
  • The closure of the Belgium facility was highlighted as a necessary step to address structural costs, with production shifted to Morocco and Pennsylvania, minimizing top-line impact but incurring a $24 million restructuring charge.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of recoupling to OEM production rates, defense growth sustainability, and the ability to secure future pricing improvements in long-term contracts.

Final Takeaway

Hexcel’s management emphasized continued operational discipline amid near-term headwinds, including A350 destocking and tariff impacts, while reaffirming confidence in the company’s long-term prospects. The outlook is anchored by robust demand in commercial aerospace and defense, ongoing cost control initiatives, and a targeted $1 billion in cumulative cash generation over the next four years. Recent contract wins, production footprint optimization, and proactive capacity management position Hexcel to benefit from the coming ramp in aircraft build rates and defense spending, with the company remaining vigilant in managing risks and capitalizing on both organic and inorganic growth opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goldman hikes forecast for already spiking diesel on tight supply
data.description:
Red-hot diesel refining margins may cool off from current highs, but likely will still end up above long-run averages given the crunch in global processing capacity, Goldman Sachs said in a new report.

Tightness in the diesel market has been a standout theme of the global petroleum market in recent months; U.S. diesel inventories remain at their lowest level since 1996 on a seasonal basis, and Singapore holdings of middle distillates - a category that includes diesel - have dropped to the lowest since February 2024.

"We expect some moderation in diesel margins from current very high levels," but they should stay "above pre-pandemic averages on continuing structural tightness in refining capacity," Goldman wrote.

Goldman sees diesel refining margins staying ~$10/bbl higher in this year's H2 and in 2026 vs. the 2013-19 average, European gasoil margins are forecast at $23/bbl compared to $19/bbl previously, and U.S. heating oil - a similar product - is projected at $28/bbl compared with $23/bbl.

The continued slowdown in the pace of global refinery capacity additions from 1.2M bbl/day in 2023-24 to 500K bbl/day in 2025-26 will keep refined-product margins elevated, Goldman said.

Crude oil futures ended the session and the week lower in rangebound trading, supported by the U.S. inching toward a trade deal with the European Union and a second weekly draw in U.S. crude oil inventories, while additional OPEC+ output kept a lid on advances.

"We're in a peak summer lull... there's just not much of an appetite for putting on significant directional risk," Societe Generale analyst Ben Hoff said in a note.

Looking ahead, analysts continue to expect a significant oversupply as seasonal demand eases in the fall, which will likely put additional pressure on prices.

This week, front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for September delivery finished -1.3% to $65.16/bbl, and front-month Brent September crude (CO1:COM) settled -1.2% to $68.44/bbl, while U.S. natural gas posted its fourth loss in five weeks, with the August front-month contract closing -12.7% to $3.110/MMBtu, pressured by forecasts of cooler than normal temperatures in the first week of August and possibly the following week.

On Friday, Nymex crude and Brent settled down 1.3% and 1.1%, while U.S. natural gas edged higher by 0.5%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLE), were +1.3% for the week.

Top 10 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Fusion Fuel Green (HTOO) +64.5%, Bloom Energy (BE) +37.4%, Maxeon Solar Technologies (MAXN) +33.1%, American Battery Technology (ABAT) +31.2%, Eco Wave Power (WAVE) +28.9%, Geospace Technologies (GEOS) +20.4%, Atlas Lithium (ATLX) +20.3%, Lithium Argentina (LAR) +18.3%, Uranium Royalty (UROY) +18.3%, Borr Drilling (BORR) +17.5%.

data.title: Shell to move forward on Mina West gas project in Egypt's Mediterranean waters
data.description:
Shell (NYSE:SHEL) has taken a final investment decision on the development of the Mina West offshore natural gas field in Egypt's Mediterranean waters, OilPrice.com reported Friday, a move that comes as Egypt struggles with dependence on liquefied natural gas import and declining production from legacy fields.

Shell (NYSE:SHEL) said Mina West, which contains an estimated 500B cf of gas, will be developed via a subsea tie-back to existing West Delta Deep Marine infrastructure, allowing accelerated production at lower emissions intensity.

The Mina West project is expected to deliver early gas volumes to Egypt’s domestic grid while supporting Shell's (SHEL) goal of expanding integrated gas production by 1% annually through the decade, according to OilPrice.com.

Shell (SHEL) holds a 60% operating stake in Mina West through its subsidiary BG International, and Kuwait Foreign Petroleum Exploration Company controls the remaining 40%, developing the field in collaboration with the Egyptian Natural Gas Holding Company.

data.title: Atai Life Sciences down 12% after hours on mid-stage failure of schizophrenia asset
data.description:
• Atai Life Sciences (NASDAQ:ATAI) is off ~12% in after-hours trading Friday after a company it has a significant investment in reported that a phase 2b study of a schizophrenia asset missed its primary endpoint. 
  • Recognify Life Sciences said that inidascamine did not achieve statistical significance on a scale of improvement in cognitive and functional measures compared to placebo. 
  • Inidascamine, an oral drug, works by modulating cholinergic, glutamatergic and GABA-B receptors, "thereby putatively altering the excitatory/inhibitory balance in the brain to produce pro-cognitive effects," according to Recognify.
  • The trial enrolled individuals with cognitive impairment associated with schizophrenia.
  • "While we believe these results support the continued development of inidascamine by Recognify for CIAS as well as its potential application in other indications, as previously communicated, we intend to allocate atai’s resources on our wholly owned pipeline of transformative psychedelic product candidates focused on affective disorders," Atai CEO Srinivas Rao said in a news release.
